evidence: Ivor's framing: 'The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.'
V: 'Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.'
Ivor immediately tracked ('yeah, 100%') when V articulated the territorial lock-in thesis

V: 'I've become so black pilled on the idea that we are so limited by the ATS and the realities and the practical limits of how we process talent at scale that it's hard to even conceive of a world where you could have actual insight into how someone thinks before talking to them.'
V: 'Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.'

V: 'The biggest change was really the realization that our hypothesis was correct about it being the correct modality for capturing human data. And I stand by that. And that's still a part of the product.'
Ivor: 'You should go and talk to Handshake, dude... We've got a new kind of like Head of Strategic Ops joining week after next. I'll get her to reach out.'

source_display: The 'Quiet Part' Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Discussion of employers having selection criteria they cannot legally state - the gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences as a structural feature of hiring markets

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: V to Ilse: 'What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters.'
Indian recruiter quote: 'I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.' - Commoditization already happened; AI just made it visible

source_display: Response Rate as the Only Metric That Matters
evidence: Logan's insight: use response rates as the convincing proxy — 'our users pay attention to our recommendations'

source_display: Sora Video as Product Vision Articulation
evidence: Ilse's recollection of a generated Sora video—an auditorium full of candidates that empties to three people after clicking a Careerspan button—capturing: 'hiring is too loud, and we make it quiet'
Superposition wouldn't bring users. They might not even bring significant revenue. But their logo creates a trust cascade. This is pure signaling economics—the brand IS the product.
V's counter to 'you only have 500 users': 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' - Inverting network effects playbook to exclusivity positioning
Logan's biotech friend disrupted her industry by going directly to morgues instead of through middlemen. 'My middleman is charging me too much money' → go direct to source.
Decision: Pursue both brand-name partnerships (Superposition) for credibility AND hungry individual recruiters for execution. Owner: Logan (individual recruiters)
Decision: Prioritize NYC for high-signal user acquisition. Channels: Harvard Club of NYC, Cornell Club of NYC, AI Circle (via Albert), NYC-specific alumni networks.
